Insurance Billing Code Reference
ICD-10 Diagnosis Codes, Procedure Codes & Insurer Package Rates
MediAssist Health Network Central Billing Office Document ref: BILL-CODE-010 · Version 8.0 Access: Billing Executives & Admin

Introduction
This reference maps the diagnosis and procedure codes used in MediAssist claim submissions to insurer package rates. Accurate coding is the single biggest determinant of timely claim approval.
- ICD-10 codes describe the diagnosis (why the patient was treated).
- Procedure codes describe the intervention (what was done).
- Specificity matters: an unspecified code invites a query or rejection. Always code to the highest level of detail supported by the clinical record.

Note
Package rates below are indicative MediAssist negotiated rates. Final settlement depends on the patient's policy, sum insured, co-pay and sub-limits.

4. Exclusions Reference
The reasons below are the most common grounds for claim rejection. Each carries an exclusion code used in the rejection letter; see the Claim Submission & Escalation Guide for the matching response template.
EXCL-01, Exclusion / Rejection Reason = Non-disclosure of pre-existing condition. EXCL-01, Typical Outcome = Appealable with prior records. EXCL-02, Exclusion / Rejection Reason = Treatment within policy waiting period. EXCL-02, Typical Outcome = Usually upheld; verify dates. EXCL-03, Exclusion / Rejection Reason = Treatment by non-empanelled provider. EXCL-03, Typical Outcome = Reimbursement route only. EXCL-04, Exclusion / Rejection Reason = Day-care procedure claimed as inpatient. EXCL-04, Typical Outcome = Re-file under correct package. EXCL-05, Exclusion / Rejection Reason = Cosmetic / aesthetic procedure. EXCL-05, Typical Outcome = Excluded; usually final. EXCL-06, Exclusion / Rejection Reason = Self-inflicted injury. EXCL-06, Typical Outcome = Excluded per policy. EXCL-07, Exclusion / Rejection Reason = Investigation-only admission (no treatment). EXCL-07, Typical Outcome = Often disallowed. EXCL-08, Exclusion / Rejection Reason = Documentation beyond claim deadline. EXCL-08, Typical Outcome = Appeal with justification

4. Exclusions Reference
Best practice
Coding tip: when a patient has multiple conditions, sequence the primary diagnosis (the main reason for admission) first, followed by comorbidities. Mis-sequencing is a frequent cause of package-rate disputes.

5. Room Rent & Sub-limit Reference
Most indemnity policies link the eligible room category to the sum insured. Exceeding the eligible room rent triggers proportionate deduction across the whole bill, so room selection must be counselled at admission.
General ward, Typical Eligibility = Sum insured < ₹3 lakh. General ward, Effect if Exceeded = —. Twin sharing, Typical Eligibility = Sum insured ₹3–5 lakh. Twin sharing, Effect if Exceeded = Proportionate deduction if upgraded. Single private, Typical Eligibility = Sum insured ₹5–10 lakh. Single private, Effect if Exceeded = Proportionate deduction if upgraded. Deluxe / suite, Typical Eligibility = Sum insured > ₹10 lakh or rider. Deluxe / suite, Effect if Exceeded = Often excluded; confirm sub-limit

6. Common Co-pay & Sub-limits
Senior-citizen co-pay, Typical Provision = 10–20% of admissible claim. Zone-based co-pay (metro vs non-metro), Typical Provision = As per policy schedule. Cataract sub-limit, Typical Provision = ₹25,000–₹40,000 per eye. Maternity sub-limit, Typical Provision = ₹35,000 (normal) / ₹50,000 (LSCS), where covered. Ambulance charges, Typical Provision = ₹2,000–₹5,000 per hospitalisation

7. Pre-authorisation Document Checklist
- Completed insurer pre-authorisation form
- Admission note with provisional diagnosis and ICD-10 code
- Treating doctor's treatment plan and estimated length of stay
- Cost estimate mapped to the package rate
- Patient policy/e-card, photo ID and policy number
- For accidents: MLC number and FIR copy where applicable

7. Pre-authorisation Document Checklist

Claim Submission & Escalation Guide
**MediAssist Health Network — Billing & Insurance Operations**
Document ref: BILL-OPS-011 · Version 6.0 · Owner: Central Billing Office, Hyderabad
Audience: Billing Executives, Floor Coordinators, Admin · Access:
```
billing_executive
```
,
```
admin
```

Claim Submission & Escalation Guide
Purpose & Scope
This guide is the standard operating reference for billing executives handling insurance
claims at any MediAssist hospital or clinic. It covers the four claim journeys you will
encounter daily —
**cashless pre-authorisation**
,
**reimbursement**
,
**pre-authorisation**
**enhancement**
, and
**rejection response**
— plus the escalation matrix and fraud-prevention
controls that govern all of them.
It applies to every empanelled insurer listed in
```
billing_codes.pdf
```
. Where an
insurer-specific portal or Third Party Administrator (TPA) differs from the general process,
the difference is called out explicitly.
**Golden rule:**
Every claim action must leave an audit trail. If it is not logged in the
MediAssist Billing Portal (MBP), for claim purposes it did not happen.

Claim Submission & Escalation Guide
Purpose & Scope
How claims flow at MediAssist
```
Admission ──► Eligibility check ──► Pre-auth (cashless) ──► Treatment ──► Final bill
                  │                        │                                  │
                  └─ not eligible ─► Reimbursement route        Enhancement (if bill > approved)
                                                                              │
                                          Approved ◄── Query/Rejection ◄──────┘
```

Claim Submission & Escalation Guide
1. Cashless Claim Process
Cashless is the default pathway for
**planned and emergency admissions**
where the patient
holds a policy with an empanelled insurer. The hospital is paid directly by the insurer/TPA;
the patient pays only non-covered items, co-pay and amounts above sub-limits.

1. Cashless Claim Process
Claim Submission & Escalation Guide
1. Cashless Claim Process
1.1 Pre-authorisation timeline
Planned admission, Pre-auth request deadline = At least48 hours beforeadmission. Emergency admission, Pre-auth request deadline = Within 6 hoursof admission
Missing the 6-hour emergency window is the single most common reason for avoidable cashless
denials. Raise the request even with provisional details and enhance later (see Section 3).

1. Cashless Claim Process
Claim Submission & Escalation Guide
1. Cashless Claim Process
1.2 Documents required for pre-authorisation
1. Duly filled insurer-specific pre-authorisation form (download from the insurer portal).
2. Admission note with **provisional diagnosis** and matching ICD-10 code from `billing_codes.pdf` .
3. Treating doctor's **treatment plan** and estimated length of stay (LOS).
4. Cost estimate mapped to the relevant **package rate** .
5. Patient KYC: policy card / e-card, government photo ID, and policy number.
6. For accident cases: MLC (Medico-Legal Case) number and FIR copy where applicable.

metadata.section_title: 1. Cashless Claim Process > 1.2 Documents required for pre-authorisation

text:
1. Cashless Claim Process
Claim Submission & Escalation Guide
1. Cashless Claim Process
1.3 Step-by-step
1. Verify patient eligibility on the insurer portal (policy active, sum insured available, waiting period cleared, room-rent category).
2. Counsel the patient on room eligibility — exceeding the eligible room rent triggers a **proportionate deduction** across the whole bill.
3. Create the claim in **MBP → Claims → New Cashless** and attach the documents above.
4. Submit the pre-auth request via the insurer portal **and** log the portal reference number back in MBP.
5. Record the timestamp of submission — the SLA clock starts here.
6. Track status hourly until an approval, query, or denial is received.
7. On approval, record the **approved amount** and **pre-auth number** in MBP and inform the ward.
8. On a query, respond within **2 hours** with the requested clinical documents.

1. Cashless Claim Process
Claim Submission & Escalation Guide
1. Cashless Claim Process
1.4 Typical approval turnaround (initial pre-auth)
Star Health, Standard SLA = 2–4 hrs. Star Health, Notes = Fastest; auto-adjudication for standard packages. HDFC Ergo, Standard SLA = 3–6 hrs. HDFC Ergo, Notes = Frequent clinical queries on cardiac packages. ICICI Lombard, Standard SLA = 3–6 hrs. ICICI Lombard, Notes = TPA-routed; confirm TPA before submitting. New India Assurance, Standard SLA = 4–8 hrs. New India Assurance, Notes = PSU insurer; slower on weekends. United India, Standard SLA = 4–8 hrs. United India, Notes = Manual review common. Bajaj Allianz, Standard SLA = 3–5 hrs. Bajaj Allianz, Notes = . Niva Bupa, Standard SLA = 2–5 hrs. Niva Bupa, Notes = . Care Health, Standard SLA = 3–6 hrs. Care Health, Notes = 
**If no response within the SLA window**
, escalate per the matrix in Section 5.

metadata.section_title: 1. Cashless Claim Process > 1.4 Typical approval turnaround (initial pre-auth)

text:
1. Cashless Claim Process
Claim Submission & Escalation Guide
1. Cashless Claim Process
1.5 Worked example
A patient is admitted at 9:00 pm with chest pain; provisional diagnosis
**NSTEMI (I21.4)**
,
package rate
**₹1,20,000**
, insurer
**HDFC Ergo**
. The executive raises the pre-auth by
11:30 pm (within the 6-hour window), attaching the admission note, ECG and troponin.
HDFC Ergo raises a clinical query on the troponin trend at 1:00 am; it is answered within
the 2-hour window. Approval for ₹1,10,000 (with ₹10,000 towards non-payables) lands at 4:00 am.
Total turnaround: ~4.5 hours — within SLA.

Claim Submission & Escalation Guide
2. Reimbursement Claim Process
Used when the patient
**pays the hospital directly**
and claims from the insurer afterwards —
typically when the insurer is not empanelled, the patient chose not to use cashless, or a
cashless request was declined for administrative (not clinical) reasons.

2. Reimbursement Claim Process
Claim Submission & Escalation Guide
2. Reimbursement Claim Process
2.1 Documents required
1. Original itemised hospital bills with payment receipts.
2. **Discharge summary** signed by the treating consultant.
3. Prescription copies and pharmacy invoices.
4. Investigation reports (lab, radiology) supporting the diagnosis.
5. Completed reimbursement claim form with patient **NEFT/bank details** .
6. Cancelled cheque or passbook copy for payout.
7. KYC documents and policy copy.

2. Reimbursement Claim Process
Claim Submission & Escalation Guide
2. Reimbursement Claim Process
2.2 Step-by-step
1. Counsel the patient at discharge that the claim is reimbursement, not cashless.
2. Issue the complete original document set and retain certified copies in MBP.
3. Help the patient fill the claim form; verify the ICD-10 and procedure codes match the bills.
4. Note the insurer's **submission deadline** — typically **30 days post-discharge** (confirm per insurer; some allow 15, some 90).
5. Hand over a document checklist so the patient can track what they submitted, and send a written (email/SMS) reminder of the deadline.
Reimbursement claims are owned by the patient once they leave, but billing executives are
responsible for issuing a
**complete, claim-ready document set**
. Incomplete discharge
paperwork is a recurring rejection cause.

Claim Submission & Escalation Guide
3. Pre-Authorisation Enhancement
Raise an
**enhancement**
when the initially approved amount will not cover the actual
treatment cost — e.g. the patient needed ICU escalation, an additional procedure, or a
longer LOS than estimated.

3. Pre-Authorisation Enhancement
Claim Submission & Escalation Guide
3. Pre-Authorisation Enhancement
3.1 When to raise
- Projected final bill exceeds the approved pre-auth amount by more than **10%** .
- A new procedure or implant (e.g. stent, prosthesis) is added mid-admission.
- LOS extends beyond the approved package days.

3. Pre-Authorisation Enhancement
Claim Submission & Escalation Guide
3. Pre-Authorisation Enhancement
3.2 Documents required
1. Enhancement request form referencing the **original pre-auth number** .
2. **Clinical justification** note from the treating doctor explaining the change.
3. Revised cost estimate and updated treatment plan.
4. Any new investigation reports supporting the escalation.

3. Pre-Authorisation Enhancement
Claim Submission & Escalation Guide
3. Pre-Authorisation Enhancement
3.3 Process
1. Raise the enhancement **before** the original approved amount is exhausted — never after discharge.
2. Submit via the insurer portal linked to the original pre-auth, clearly marked "Enhancement".
3. Track as a child claim under the parent pre-auth in MBP.
4. If the enhancement is delayed beyond **6 hours** and the patient is due for discharge, escalate to the insurer nodal officer (Section 5).

Claim Submission & Escalation Guide
4. Claim Rejection Response
A rejection is not the end of the claim. Most rejections on
**administrative or documentation**
**grounds**
are reversible on appeal. Rejection codes here map to the exclusion codes in
```
billing_codes.pdf
```
.

4. Claim Rejection Response
Claim Submission & Escalation Guide
4. Claim Rejection Response
4.1 Common rejection codes
EXCL-01, Reason = Non-disclosure of pre-existing condition. EXCL-01, First response = Submit prior medical records / declaration. EXCL-02, Reason = Treatment within waiting period. EXCL-02, First response = Verify policy dates; appeal if mis-calculated. EXCL-03, Reason = Non-empanelled treating doctor / provider. EXCL-03, First response = Provide registration proof; or move to reimbursement. EXCL-04, Reason = Day-care procedure claimed as inpatient. EXCL-04, First response = Re-submit under correct day-care package. EXCL-05, Reason = Cosmetic / excluded procedure. EXCL-05, First response = Usually final; confirm exclusion in policy. EXCL-07, Reason = Investigation-only admission. EXCL-07, First response = Provide clinical necessity note. EXCL-08, Reason = Documentation beyond deadline. EXCL-08, First response = Appeal with justification for delay

metadata.section_title: 4. Claim Rejection Response > 4.1 Common rejection codes

text:
4. Claim Rejection Response
Claim Submission & Escalation Guide
4. Claim Rejection Response
4.2 Counter-response template
**Subject:**
Reconsideration request — Claim [CLM-2024-XXXX] / Pre-auth [number]
Dear [Insurer/TPA],
We request reconsideration of the above claim rejected under code [EXCL-0X] dated [date].
Please find attached [document] which addresses the stated reason. The treating consultant's
clarification is enclosed. We request review within the reconsideration window.
Regards, MediAssist Billing — [Campus].

4. Claim Rejection Response
Claim Submission & Escalation Guide
4. Claim Rejection Response
4.3 Deadlines
- Reconsideration / appeal must be filed within the insurer's window — **usually 90 days** from rejection.
- If the appeal is denied, escalate to the **insurer grievance cell** , then the **Insurance** **Ombudsman** (Section 5).

Claim Submission & Escalation Guide
5. Escalation Matrix
Use this matrix when an SLA is breached or a claim stalls. Always escalate
**in writing**
and
log each step in MBP.
Cashless pre-auth delayed, First Contact = TPA helpline. Cashless pre-auth delayed, Escalation (24 hrs no response) = Insurer nodal officer. Cashless pre-auth delayed, Final Escalation = MediAssist billing manager. Claim rejected — clinical grounds, First Contact = Medical reviewer call. Claim rejected — clinical grounds, Escalation (24 hrs no response) = Written appeal with CMO sign-off. Claim rejected — clinical grounds, Final Escalation = Insurance ombudsman. Payment delayed >30 days post-discharge, First Contact = Accounts team. Payment delayed >30 days post-discharge, Escalation (24 hrs no response) = CFO office. Payment delayed >30 days post-discharge, Final Escalation = Legal cell. Enhancement not approved before discharge, First Contact = TPA helpline. Enhancement not approved before discharge, Escalation (24 hrs no response) = Insurer nodal officer. Enhancement not approved before discharge, Final Escalation = MediAssist billing manager
**Escalation discipline:**
- Wait the stated window before moving to the next level — premature escalation resets goodwill.
- Every escalation email must quote the claim ID, pre-auth number, patient ID, and the SLA breached.
- CC the campus billing manager on any escalation beyond first contact.
- Never let more than **24 hours** pass without an escalation when a response is overdue.

Claim Submission & Escalation Guide
6. Fraud Prevention
Claim fraud — internal or external — is a zero-tolerance area governed by the Code of Conduct.
Billing executives are the first line of defence.

6. Fraud Prevention
Claim Submission & Escalation Guide
6. Fraud Prevention
6.1 Warning signs
- Mismatch between the diagnosis, the procedure, and the investigation reports.
- Inflated or duplicated line items on the bill.
- Documents that appear altered, back-dated, or inconsistent in handwriting/fonts.
- Pressure from any party to "adjust" codes to a higher package rate (up-coding).
- Repeated claims for the same patient across facilities within a short period.

6. Fraud Prevention
Claim Submission & Escalation Guide
6. Fraud Prevention
6.2 Controls
- **Mandatory dual-check** for any claim above **₹2,00,000** — a second billing executive must independently verify codes, amounts, and documents before submission.
- Every claim must carry a complete **audit trail** in MBP: who created, edited, submitted, and approved each step, with timestamps.
- Claim files are retained for a minimum of **7 years** .
- Never share insurer portal credentials; each executive uses their own login.

6. Fraud Prevention
Claim Submission & Escalation Guide
6. Fraud Prevention
6.3 Reporting
Suspected fraud must be reported to the
**Billing Manager and Internal Audit within 24 hours**
via MBP → Compliance → Report Concern (or compliance@mediassist.in). Reports may be made
confidentially. Retaliation against good-faith reporters is itself a disciplinary offence.

Claim Submission & Escalation Guide
7. Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)
Billing executives are measured monthly on the following. Targets are indicative.
Pre-auth raised within SLA, Target = > 95%. Pre-auth raised within SLA, Why it matters = Drives same-day approvals. Query response time, Target = < 2 hrs. Query response time, Why it matters = Prevents avoidable rejections. First-pass approval rate, Target = > 85%. First-pass approval rate, Why it matters = Reflects coding & documentation quality. Claim rejection rate, Target = < 10%. Claim rejection rate, Why it matters = Quality and compliance indicator. Average days to payment, Target = < 21 days. Average days to payment, Why it matters = Cash-flow health. Reopened/appealed claims won, Target = > 60%. Reopened/appealed claims won, Why it matters = Effectiveness of escalation

Diagnostic Reference Guide
Quick Reference for Laboratory Values and Imaging Findings
MediAssist Health Network
Central Laboratory & Radiology
Document ref: LAB-DIAG-007 · Version 6.1 Access: Doctors and Admin
This guide supports rapid interpretation of common laboratory values and imaging findings. Reference ranges follow MediAssist central laboratory standards; critical values trigger the notification protocol in the final section.

1. Haematology Reference Ranges
Haemoglobin (male), Normal Range = 13–17 g/dL. Haemoglobin (male), Critical Value = < 7 g/dL. Haemoglobin (male), Action = Activate transfusion protocol. Haemoglobin (female), Normal Range = 12–15 g/dL. Haemoglobin (female), Critical Value = < 7 g/dL. Haemoglobin (female), Action = Activate transfusion protocol. WBC count, Normal Range = 4–11 × 10⁹/L. WBC count, Critical Value = > 30 × 10⁹/L. WBC count, Action = Haematology consult. Platelet count, Normal Range = 150–400 × 10⁹/L. Platelet count, Critical Value = < 50 × 10⁹/L. Platelet count, Action = Bleeding-risk protocol. PT / INR, Normal Range = INR 0.9–1.2. PT / INR, Critical Value = INR > 5. PT / INR, Action = Withhold anticoagulant; review. aPTT, Normal Range = 26–36 s. aPTT, Critical Value = > 90 s. aPTT, Action = Review heparin; bleeding risk. D-dimer, Normal Range = < 0.5 µg/mL. D-dimer, Critical Value = Marked rise. D-dimer, Action = Consider VTE; correlate clinically. Ferritin, Normal Range = 30–300 ng/mL. Ferritin, Critical Value = —. Ferritin, Action = Low: iron deficiency; high: inflammation. ESR, Normal Range = M < 15, F < 20 mm/hr. ESR, Critical Value = —. ESR, Action = Non-specific inflammation marker

2. Biochemistry Reference Ranges

Sodium, Normal Range = 135–145 mEq/L. Sodium, Critical / Significant = < 120 or > 160. Sodium, Note = Correct slowly. Potassium, Normal Range = 3.5–5.0 mEq/L. Potassium, Critical / Significant = < 3.0 or > 6.0. Potassium, Note = Cardiac monitoring if extreme. Creatinine, Normal Range = 0.6–1.3 mg/dL. Creatinine, Critical / Significant = Rapid rise. Creatinine, Note = Assess AKI; review nephrotoxics. Urea, Normal Range = 15–45 mg/dL. Urea, Critical / Significant = —. Urea, Note = Correlate with creatinine. Glucose (fasting), Normal Range = 70–100 mg/dL. Glucose (fasting), Critical / Significant = < 50 or > 400. Glucose (fasting), Note = Treat hypo-/hyperglycaemia. HbA1c, Normal Range = < 5.7%. HbA1c, Critical / Significant = ≥ 6.5% = diabetes. HbA1c, Note = Glycaemic control over ~3 months. ALT / AST, Normal Range = < 40 U/L. ALT / AST, Critical / Significant = > 10× ULN. ALT / AST, Note = Hepatocellular injury. ALP, Normal Range = 40–130 U/L. ALP, Critical / Significant = —. ALP, Note = Cholestasis / bone. Bilirubin (total), Normal Range = 0.3–1.2 mg/dL. Bilirubin (total), Critical / Significant = —. Bilirubin (total), Note = Jaundice if elevated. Troponin I, Normal Range = < 0.04 ng/mL. Troponin I, Critical / Significant = > 0.4 = significant. Troponin I, Note = Serial; correlate with ECG. CRP, Normal Range = < 5 mg/L. CRP, Critical / Significant = Marked rise. CRP, Note = Inflammation / infection. Procalcitonin, Normal Range = < 0.5 ng/mL. Procalcitonin, Critical / Significant = > 2 ng/mL. Procalcitonin, Note = Suggests

2. Biochemistry Reference Ranges
 bacterial sepsis

3. Urine Analysis Interpretation
Protein +, Interpretation = Possible renal disease / UTI. Protein +, Next Step = Quantify (ACR); repeat. Glucose +, Interpretation = Hyperglycaemia / low renal threshold. Glucose +, Next Step = Check blood glucose. Blood +, Interpretation = Haematuria — infection, stones, malignancy. Blood +, Next Step = Microscopy; imaging if persistent. Nitrites +, Interpretation = Gram-negative bacteriuria. Nitrites +, Next Step = Send culture; treat if symptomatic. Leucocytes +, Interpretation = Pyuria / inflammation. Leucocytes +, Next Step = Correlate with nitrites & symptoms. Nitrites + & Leucocytes +, Interpretation = Strongly suggests UTI. Nitrites + & Leucocytes +, Next Step = Treat per antibiogram

4. ECG Interpretation Quick Guide
Rate, Normal = 60–100 bpm. Rate, Abnormal Significance = < 60 brady; > 100 tachy. Rhythm, Normal = Sinus. Rhythm, Abnormal Significance = AF, flutter, VT, SVT — classify. P-R interval, Normal = 120–200 ms. P-R interval, Abnormal Significance = > 200 ms = 1° AV block. QRS duration, Normal = < 120 ms. QRS duration, Abnormal Significance = > 120 ms = bundle branch block. QTc, Normal = < 440 ms (M), < 460 ms (F). QTc, Abnormal Significance = Prolonged → arrhythmia risk. ST elevation, Normal = Isoelectric. ST elevation, Abnormal Significance = > 1 mm in ≥ 2 contiguous leads = STEMI protocol
Common arrhythmias: AF — irregularly irregular, absent P waves; VT — broad-complex, regular, > 100 bpm, medical emergency; SVT — narrow-complex tachycardia, often regular.

5. Arterial Blood Gas (ABG) Interpretation
pH, Normal Range = 7.35–7.45. pH, Interpretation = < 7.35 acidosis; > 7.45 alkalosis. PaCO₂, Normal Range = 35–45 mmHg. PaCO₂, Interpretation = Respiratory component. HCO₃⁻, Normal Range = 22–26 mEq/L. HCO₃⁻, Interpretation = Metabolic component. PaO₂, Normal Range = 80–100 mmHg. PaO₂, Interpretation = < 60 = respiratory failure. Lactate, Normal Range = 0.5–2.2 mmol/L. Lactate, Interpretation = > 4 suggests hypoperfusion / sepsis
Quick approach: assess pH → identify primary disorder (respiratory vs metabolic) from PaCO₂ and HCO₃⁻ → check for compensation → calculate the anion gap for metabolic acidosis.

6. Thyroid & Cardiac/Sepsis Markers
TSH, Normal Range = 0.4–4.0 mIU/L. TSH, Note = First-line thyroid screen. Free T4, Normal Range = 0.8–1.8 ng/dL. Free T4, Note = Confirms thyroid status. BNP / NT-proBNP, Normal Range = Age-dependent. BNP / NT-proBNP, Note = Elevated in heart failure. CK-MB, Normal Range = < 5 ng/mL. CK-MB, Note = Cardiac injury (older marker). Lactate (sepsis), Normal Range = < 2 mmol/L. Lactate (sepsis), Note = ≥ 2 triggers sepsis-6 review

7. Radiology Reporting Flags
Chest X-ray (CXR), Urgent means = Pneumothorax, free air, mis-placed line. Chest X-ray (CXR), Routine TAT = Reported within 24 h. CT head, Urgent means = Bleed, mass effect, acute infarct. CT head, Routine TAT = Reported within 12 h. USS abdomen, Urgent means = Free fluid, obstruction, aneurysm. USS abdomen, Routine TAT = Reported within 24 h

8. Microbiology & Culture Turnaround
Blood culture, Preliminary = Gram stain at flagging (~12–24 h). Blood culture, Final Report = 48–72 h (5 days if negative). Urine culture, Preliminary = —. Urine culture, Final Report = 24–48 h. Sputum culture, Preliminary = Gram stain same day. Sputum culture, Final Report = 48–72 h. CSF analysis, Preliminary = Urgent Gram stain < 1 h. CSF analysis, Final Report = Culture 48–72 h. Stool culture, Preliminary = —. Stool culture, Final Report = 48–72 h
Antibiotic sensitivities accompany the final report; clinicians should de-escalate empirical therapy once the antibiogram is available.

9. Tumour Markers (interpret with caution)
Marker, 1 = Associated With. Marker, 2 = Note. PSA, 1 = Prostate. PSA, 2 = Age-specific; not for screening alone
CA-125, Associated With = Ovarian. CA-125, Note = Raised in many benign conditions. CEA, Associated With = Colorectal. CEA, Note = Trend more useful than single value. AFP, Associated With = Hepatocellular / germ cell. AFP, Note = Correlate with imaging

9. Tumour Markers (interpret with caution)
Critical values
The laboratory must telephone the treating ward/doctor within 15 minutes of a critical value. The doctor must acknowledge and document the action within 30 minutes. If the doctor is unreachable, the lab escalates to the duty registrar, then the on-call consultant.

The MediAssist Approved Drug Formulary lists medicines approved for use across all hospitals, with standard dosing, storage, formulary tier and substitution rules. Prescribers must select from this formulary; out-of-formulary requests follow the process in the final section.

Formulary Tier System
Every medicine in this formulary carries a tier that governs how it is authorised:
- Tier 1 — generic first-line; open to all prescribers and subsidised for staff.
- Tier 2 — branded preferred; open to prescribers.
- Tier 3 — specialist use; requires HOD approval.
- Tier 4 — restricted; requires CMO approval.

 Many drug interactions. Meropenem, Class = Carbapenem. Meropenem, Route = IV. Meropenem, Standard Dose = 1 g Q8H. Meropenem, Tier = 3. Meropenem, Storage = Room temp. Fentanyl, Controlled? = Yes — narcotic register. Buprenorphine, Route(s) = Sublingual / patch. Buprenorphine, Dose Range = Per pain protocol. Buprenorphine, Controlled? = Yes (schedule). 4. Respiratory Drugs
Salbutamol, Route = Inhaled / Neb. Salbutamol, Standard Dose = 100–200 µg PRN / 2.5–5 mg neb. Salbutamol, Tier = 1. Salbutamol, Notes = Tremor, tachycardia. Ipratropium, Route = Inhaled / Neb. 6. Renal Dose Adjustment (selected drugs)
Doses below are illustrative; confirm against the latest renal dosing reference and the patient's eGFR before prescribing.
Metformin, eGFR 30–50 = Max 1 g/day; review. Metformin, eGFR < 30 = Avoid. Meropenem, eGFR 30–50 = Reduce frequency to Q12H. Meropenem, eGFR < 30 = Q24H; specialist input. Vancomycin, eGFR 30–50 = Extend interval per TDM. Vancomycin, eGFR < 30 = Per TDM; close monitoring. Enoxaparin, eGFR 30–50 = Standard with monitoring. Enoxaparin, eGFR < 30 = Reduce to OD; consider UFH. Digoxin, eGFR 30–50 = Reduce dose; check levels. Digoxin, eGFR < 30 = Marked reduction; toxicity risk

6. Renal Dose Adjustment (selected drugs)
High-alert
The medicines below carry a heightened risk of significant patient harm when used in error. They require an independent double-check by two staff before administration and are restricted to trained personnel.
Insulin, Special Storage = Refrigerate 2–8 °C. Insulin, Key Safeguard = Units never abbreviated as 'U'; double-check dose. Heparin, Special Storage = Room temp; separate from insulin. Heparin, Key Safeguard = Double-check; aPTT monitoring. Potassium Chloride (conc.), Special Storage = Locked; never on open shelf. 8. Substitution Policy
- When a Tier 2 drug is unavailable, the pharmacist may substitute the equivalent Tier 1 generic without a prescription change.
- Tier 3 / Tier 4 substitutions require the prescribing doctor's written approval.
- All substitutions are logged in the pharmacy system and visible on the patient record.

9. Out-of-Formulary Request Process
When a required medicine is not on the formulary, follow these steps:
- 1 The prescriber submits an out-of-formulary (OOF) request form with clinical justification.
- 2 The Pharmacy & Therapeutics committee reviews the request within 72 hours.
- 3 For urgent or life-saving cases, the CMO may approve the same day.

9. Out-of-Formulary Request Process
Dispensing safety
LASA pairs are a leading cause of dispensing error. Confirm the full drug name, strength and patient before dispensing; 'Tall Man' lettering is used on labels.
DOPamine, Confused with = DOBUTamine. DOPamine, Distinguishing note = Different haemodynamic effect; confirm infusion label. epHEDrine, Confused with = epINEPHrine. epHEDrine, Distinguishing note = Very different potency; check ampoule. ceFAZolin, Confused with = cefTRIAXone. ceFAZolin, Distinguishing note = Confirm spectrum and indication. hydrOXYzine, Confused with = hydrALAZINE. hydrOXYzine, Distinguishing note = Antihistamine vs antihypertensive. clonazePAM, Confused with = cloNIDine. clonazePAM, Distinguishing note = Anticonvulsant vs antihypertensive

Standard Treatment Protocols
Clinical Reference — First-Line Management Guidelines
MediAssist Health Network Clinical Governance Committee Document ref: CLIN-PROT-005 · Version 7.0 Access: Doctors and Admin
This document sets out first-line management guidelines for the most common conditions presenting at MediAssist hospitals. Protocols are intended for use by registered medical practitioners and must be applied with clinical judgement. ICD-10 codes are provided to align clinical documentation with billing.

A. Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus
ICD-10: E11

A. Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus
Diagnostic criteria
- Fasting plasma glucose (FPG) ≥126 mg/dL, or
- 2-hour plasma glucose ≥200 mg/dL on a 75 g OGTT, or
- HbA1c ≥6.5% on a standardised assay.

metadata.section_title: A. Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus > Diagnostic criteria

text:
A. Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus
Pharmacological management
First-line, Drug & Dose = Metformin 500 mg BD with meals. First-line, Notes = Titrate to 1000 mg BD over 4 weeks. Second-line, Drug & Dose = Add Glipizide 5 mg OD. Second-line, Notes = If HbA1c > 7.5% after 3 months on Metformin. Third-line, Drug & Dose = Consider SGLT2 inhibitor / GLP-1 agonist. Third-line, Notes = Specialist-guided; review renal status

metadata.section_title: A. Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus > Pharmacological management

text:
A. Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus
Monitoring
- HbA1c every 3 months until stable, then 6-monthly.
- Fasting blood glucose weekly for the first month after any dose change.
- Renal function (eGFR) and urine ACR annually; annual fundus and foot screening.

A. Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus
Referral criteria
- HbA1c > 10% despite dual therapy → endocrinology referral.
- eGFR < 45 mL/min/1.73m² → nephrology referral and Metformin dose review.

metadata.section_title: A. Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus > Referral criteria

text:
B. Hypertension — Stage 2
ICD-10: I10

B. Hypertension — Stage 2
Diagnostic criteria
Systolic BP ≥140 mmHg or diastolic BP ≥90 mmHg confirmed on two separate readings at least one week apart, with out-of-office confirmation where feasible.

B. Hypertension — Stage 2
Pharmacological management
First-line, Drug & Dose = Amlodipine 5 mg OD. First-line, Cautions = Titrate to 10 mg if target not met in 4 weeks; ankle oedema. Second-line, Drug & Dose = Add Telmisartan 40 mg OD. Second-line, Cautions = Avoid in pregnancy; monitor K⁺ and creatinine. Add-on, Drug & Dose = Hydrochlorothiazide 12.5–25 mg OD. Add-on, Cautions = Monitor electrolytes; caution in gout

B. Hypertension — Stage 2
Monitoring
- BP check at every visit; home BP diary encouraged.
- Serum creatinine and potassium at baseline and 4 weeks after ACE-i/ARB initiation.

B. Hypertension — Stage 2
Lifestyle modification targets
- Dietary sodium — less than 2 g/day.
- Body mass index — target BMI below 25 kg/m².
- Alcohol — restrict to no more than 2 units/day.
- Aerobic exercise — at least 30 minutes, 5 days a week.

metadata.section_title: B. Hypertension — Stage 2 > Lifestyle modification targets

text:
C. Community-Acquired Pneumonia
ICD-10: J18.9

C. Community-Acquired Pneumonia
Severity scoring — CURB-65
One point each for: Confusion, Urea > 7 mmol/L, Respiratory rate ≥ 30/min, BP < 90/60 mmHg, Age ≥ 65 years. Disposition by total score:
- Score 0–1 — outpatient management.
- Score 2 — hospital admission (general ward).
- Score ≥ 3 — consider ICU / HDU care.

metadata.section_title: C. Community-Acquired Pneumonia > Severity scoring — CURB-65

text:
C. Community-Acquired Pneumonia
Antimicrobial therapy
Outpatient, Regimen = Amoxicillin 500 mg TDS. Outpatient, Duration = 5 days. Outpatient (atypical), Regimen = Add Azithromycin 500 mg OD. Outpatient (atypical), Duration = 3 days. Inpatient, Regimen = IV Piperacillin-Tazobactam 4.5 g Q8H. Inpatient, Duration = Switch to oral when afebrile > 48 h & CRP falling

C. Community-Acquired Pneumonia
Monitoring
- SpO₂ monitoring every 4 hours during admission; target 94–98%.
- Repeat chest X-ray at 6 weeks post-discharge to confirm resolution.

D. Acute Myocardial Infarction — NSTEMI
ICD-10: I21.4

D. Acute Myocardial Infarction — NSTEMI
Immediate management (first 60 minutes)
- Aspirin 300 mg loading dose stat (chewed).
- Clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose (or Ticagrelor per cardiology).
- IV Heparin 60 units/kg bolus (maximum 4000 units), then infusion per protocol.
- 12-lead ECG within 10 minutes of arrival; high-sensitivity troponin at 0 and 3 hours.

metadata.section_title: D. Acute Myocardial Infarction — NSTEMI > Immediate management (first 60 minutes)

text:
D. Acute Myocardial Infarction — NSTEMI
Risk stratification
Calculate the TIMI risk score. A score ≥ 3 indicates high risk and warrants an early invasive strategy (coronary angiography within 24 hours) .

metadata.section_title: D. Acute Myocardial Infarction — NSTEMI > Risk stratification

text:
D. Acute Myocardial Infarction — NSTEMI
Ongoing medications

E. Paediatric Fever Management
ICD-10: A90 (dengue); A91 (dengue haemorrhagic fever)
Dengue is endemic across MediAssist's catchment, peaking in the post-monsoon months. Management is supportive; the priority is recognising warning signs and plasma leakage early.

metadata.section_title: E. Paediatric Fever Management > ICD-10: A90 (dengue); A91 (dengue haemorrhagic fever)

text:
E. Paediatric Fever Management
Classification
Dengue without warning signs, Features = Fever + 2 of: nausea, rash, aches, leucopenia. Dengue without warning signs, Disposition = Outpatient with daily review. Dengue with warning signs, Features = Abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, mucosal bleed, lethargy, ↑ HCT with ↓ platelets. Dengue with warning signs, Disposition = Admit for IV fluids. Severe dengue, Features = Shock, fluid accumulation with respiratory distress, severe bleeding, organ impairment. Severe dengue, Disposition = ICU management

E. Paediatric Fever Management
Management
- Paracetamol for fever; avoid NSAIDs and Aspirin (bleeding risk).
- Isotonic fluids (0.9% saline / Ringer's lactate) titrated to haematocrit and urine output.
- Monitor platelet count and haematocrit every 12–24 hours; watch for a rising HCT with falling platelets (plasma leakage).
- Platelet transfusion only for significant bleeding or platelets < 10 × 10⁹/L.

E. Paediatric Fever Management
Red flags
Warning signs of plasma leakage warrant immediate admission and IV fluid resuscitation. A narrowing pulse pressure (< 20 mmHg) signals impending shock.

The interactions below recur across the protocols in this document and warrant specific attention at prescribing.
Azithromycin + other QTc-prolonging drugs, Risk = Prolonged QTc, arrhythmia. Azithromycin + other QTc-prolonging drugs, Action = Review ECG; avoid stacking. ACE-i/ARB + Spironolactone, Risk = Hyperkalaemia. ACE-i/ARB + Spironolactone, Action = Monitor potassium closely. Metformin + iodinated contrast, Risk = Lactic acidosis if AKI. Metformin + iodinated contrast, Action = Withhold around contrast if eGFR low. Warfarin + Metronidazole/Macrolide, Risk = ↑ INR, bleeding. Warfarin + Metronidazole/Macrolide, Action = Monitor INR; dose-adjust. NSAIDs + ACE-i + Diuretic ('triple whammy'), Risk = Acute kidney injury. NSAIDs + ACE-i + Diuretic ('triple whammy'), Action = Avoid combination. Clopidogrel + high-dose PPI, Risk = ↓ antiplatelet effect. Clopidogrel + high-dose PPI, Action = Prefer pantoprazole

Record-keeping

G. Commissioning & Acceptance Testing
Every new or repaired device must pass commissioning before clinical use. The biomedical team records the result against the asset tag in the CMMS, checking that:
- Electrical safety — earth leakage is within IEC 60601 limits.
- Functional test — all parameters read within manufacturer tolerance.
- Calibration certificate — is valid and on file.
- Asset tagging — the EQ-CAMPUS-XXXX label is applied and registered.
- User training — ward staff are trained and the sign-off is recorded.

G. Commissioning & Acceptance Testing
Mandatory
No device may be handed to biomedical engineering or an external service engineer until it has been decontaminated and a decontamination certificate attached. This protects service staff from exposure.
- Surface-clean per the device section above (70% IPA unless contraindicated).
- Visibly soiled equipment must be cleaned and, where applicable, disinfected before transport.
- Attach the completed decontamination certificate to the device and the ticket.

I. Common Operator Errors & Troubleshooting
No SpO₂ reading, Likely Cause = Probe placement / perfusion. No SpO₂ reading, First Check = Reposition probe; warm the digit. NIBP repeatedly retries, Likely Cause = Cuff size / patient movement. NIBP repeatedly retries, First Check = Use correct cuff size; keep limb still. Infusion not starting, Likely Cause = Door not latched / clamp closed. Infusion not starting, First Check = Close door; open the roller clamp. Autoclave cycle aborts, Likely Cause = Overloaded chamber / wet load. Autoclave cycle aborts, First Check = Reduce load; ensure correct packing. X-ray panel image blank, Likely Cause = Panel not paired / low battery. X-ray panel image blank, First Check = Re-pair DR panel; charge battery

Code of Conduct & Ethics Policy
Behavioural Standards, Conflict of Interest and Patient Privacy
MediAssist Health Network
Office of Compliance & Human Resources Document ref: COMP-COC-003 · Version 5.2 Applies to: All staff and contractors

1. Professional Conduct
Every MediAssist employee is expected to act with professionalism at all times. This includes punctuality, respectful communication, and respect for the clinical and administrative hierarchy.

1. Professional Conduct
Zero tolerance
MediAssist maintains zero tolerance for verbal or physical abuse directed at patients, visitors or colleagues. Substantiated abuse is treated as gross misconduct.

2. Patient Confidentiality
- Patient records, diagnoses and treatment details must never be disclosed outside authorised clinical and billing channels.
- Staff are bound by HIPAA-equivalent obligations under the Indian IT Act and applicable data-protection rules.
- Photographing patients, their records or their charts on personal devices is strictly prohibited.

3. Conflict of Interest
- Staff must not refer patients to external facilities, laboratories or pharmacies in which they hold a financial interest.
- Doctors must disclose any relationship with pharmaceutical companies (speaking fees, sponsorships, advisory roles) to the Office of Compliance annually.

4. Social Media Policy
- No posting of patient images, case details or identifiable information on any platform.
- No disparagement of MediAssist, colleagues, or competitor hospitals online.
- Personal opinions must not be presented as the official position of MediAssist.

5. Gifts & Gratuity
Gifts from patients or vendors are governed by value thresholds:
- Gifts valued up to ₹500 are permitted; use professional judgement.
- Gifts valued above ₹500 must be declared to administration.
- Gifts valued above ₹2,000 must be refused, or deposited with administration.

6. Substance Abuse
- Zero tolerance for reporting to duty under the influence of alcohol or drugs.
- Clinical staff involved in an adverse patient event may be required to undergo mandatory testing as part of the investigation.

7. Anti-Harassment Policy (POSH)
- MediAssist has a constituted POSH (Prevention of Sexual Harassment) Committee at each campus, chaired by a senior woman officer with an external member.
- Complaints may be filed in writing or by email to posh@mediassist.in.
- Investigations are completed within a 15-day internal timeline (statutory limit 90 days); penalties range up to termination.

8. Data & Systems
- No installation of unauthorised software on hospital devices.
- No sharing of login credentials under any circumstances.
- Suspected data breaches must be reported to IT Security within 2 hours of discovery.

9. Disciplinary Process
Disciplinary action follows a graduated process, proportionate to the conduct, escalating through four stages:
- 1 Verbal warning (documented).
- 2 Written warning.
- 3 Final written warning.
- 4 Termination.

9. Disciplinary Process
Gross misconduct
Gross misconduct — including patient harm, data theft, billing fraud, or assault — may result in immediate termination without progressing through the stages above, subject to due process.

10. Anti-Bribery & Anti-Corruption
- No employee may offer, solicit or accept any bribe, kickback or improper inducement in connection with referrals, procurement, billing or recruitment.
- Facilitation payments to expedite routine approvals are prohibited.
- Commissions for patient referrals ("cut practice") are strictly forbidden and may attract legal action in addition to termination.

11. Vendor & Procurement Conduct
- All vendor selection must follow the documented procurement process with at least three quotations for purchases above ₹50,000.
- Staff with a personal or family interest in a vendor must recuse themselves and declare the interest to the Office of Compliance.

12. Whistleblower Protection
MediAssist encourages staff to report genuine concerns about unethical or illegal conduct without fear of retaliation.
- Concerns may be raised confidentially to ethics@mediassist.in or through the anonymous ethics hotline.
- Good-faith reporters are protected; retaliation against a whistleblower is itself a disciplinary offence.
- Knowingly false or malicious reports, however, are treated as misconduct.

12. Whistleblower Protection
Declaration
Every employee signs an annual declaration confirming they have read, understood and will abide by this Code of Conduct. The signed declaration is retained in your personnel file on the HR Portal.

Leave & Attendance Policy
Entitlements, Application Procedures and Attendance Rules
MediAssist Health Network
Human Resources Policy Manual
Document ref: HR-LEAVE-002 · Version 4.1
Applies to: All permanent employees

1. Scope
This policy applies to all permanent employees of MediAssist Health Network across every campus and clinic. Contract and locum staff are governed by separate terms specified in their individual engagement letters. Where a statutory entitlement exceeds the provisions below, the statutory entitlement applies.

2. Leave Types and Entitlements
Leave entitlements differ between clinical staff (doctors and nurses) and non-clinical staff, reflecting roster and continuity-of-care requirements.
Earned Leave (EL), Clinical Staff = 15 days/yr. Earned Leave (EL), Non-Clinical = 18 days/yr. Earned Leave (EL), Notes = Carry forward max 30 days. Sick Leave (SL), Clinical Staff = 12 days/yr. Sick Leave (SL), Non-Clinical = 12 days/yr. Sick Leave (SL), Notes = Medical certificate beyond 3 consecutive days. Casual Leave (CL), Clinical Staff = 8 days/yr. Casual Leave (CL), Non-Clinical = 10 days/yr. Casual Leave (CL), Notes = Cannot be clubbed with EL. Maternity Leave, Clinical Staff = 182 days. Maternity Leave, Non-Clinical = 182 days. Maternity Leave, Notes = Per Maternity Benefit Act, 1961. Paternity Leave, Clinical Staff = 7 days. Paternity Leave, Non-Clinical = 7 days. Paternity Leave, Notes = Within 3 months of child's birth. Bereavement Leave, Clinical Staff = 3 days. Bereavement Leave, Non-Clinical = 3 days. Bereavement Leave, Notes = Immediate family only. Study Leave, Clinical Staff = 5 days/yr. Study Leave, Non-Clinical = Not applicable. Study Leave, Notes = CME / certification courses only. Compensatory Off, Clinical Staff = As accrued. Compensatory Off, Non-Clinical = As accrued. Compensatory Off, Notes = Avail within 60 days of accrual

3. Leave Application Process
All leave is applied for and approved through the MediAssist HR Portal. Verbal approvals are not valid. Minimum notice periods and approval routes are:
- Earned Leave (EL) — apply at least 7 days in advance; supervisor approves, escalating to the HOD if longer than 5 days.
- Casual Leave (CL) — apply at least 2 days in advance; immediate supervisor approves.
- Sick Leave (SL) — notify the same day via the app; the supervisor regularises it on your return.

3. Leave Application Process
Approval rule
Any leave longer than five consecutive days requires department head (HOD) approval, regardless of leave type.

4. Attendance Tracking
- Attendance is captured by biometric punch and the mobile check-in app (geo-fenced to campus).
- A grace period of 10 minutes applies to shift start times.
- Late arrival beyond the grace period is recorded; after 3 such occurrences in a calendar month, each further late arrival is marked as a half-day.
- Missed punches must be regularised on the HR Portal within 48 hours with supervisor approval.

5. On-Call & Duty Roster
Doctors and nurses participate in a rotational on-call roster published by the unit in-charge two weeks in advance. On-call compensation is paid per night:
- Resident doctors — ₹800 per night, doubling to ₹1,600 on Sundays and public holidays.
- Nurses — ₹500 per night, doubling to ₹1,000 on Sundays and public holidays.

6. Public Holidays
- 10 fixed national holidays plus 3 floating regional holidays per calendar year, published each January.
- Clinical staff required to work on a public holiday receive compensatory off plus 1.5× pay for that day.

7. Leave Encashment
- Accumulated Earned Leave above the 30-day carry-forward cap is encashed annually each March, calculated on basic salary.
- On separation, the full Earned Leave balance is encashed in the final settlement.
- Sick, Casual and Compensatory leave are not encashable.

8. Leave Without Pay (LOP) & Special Cases
- Leave Without Pay (LOP) is granted only when paid leave is exhausted and requires HOD plus HR approval; it does not count towards service for increments.
- Half-day leave may be availed against CL in 0.5-day units via the HR Portal.
- Sandwich rule: intervening weekly-offs and holidays between two LOP days are counted as LOP.
- Leave during probation: only Sick and Casual leave may be availed; Earned Leave accrues but is availed only after confirmation.

9. Public Holiday List (illustrative)
Ten fixed national holidays are observed each year, with regional floating holidays added per campus state. The illustrative list below is finalised and published every January:
- 1 Republic Day (26 January)
- 2 Holi
- 3 Good Friday
- 4 May Day (1 May)
- 5 Independence Day (15 August)
- 6 Ganesh Chaturthi (regional / floating)
- 7 Gandhi Jayanti (2 October)
- 8 Dussehra
- 9 Deepavali
- 10 Christmas (25 December)

9. Public Holiday List (illustrative)
Important
Absence from duty without information for 10 or more consecutive days is treated as voluntary abandonment of service and may lead to termination after due process. If you face an emergency preventing communication, inform HR at the earliest opportunity.

MediAssist Staff Handbook
A Welcome Guide for Every MediAssist Employee
MediAssist Health Network Human Resources Department Document ref: HR-HBK-001 · Version 6.0 Applies to: All staff, all campuses

1. About MediAssist Health Network
MediAssist Health Network was founded in 2008 in Hyderabad with a single 90-bed hospital and a simple conviction: that high-quality, ethical healthcare should be accessible to the communities it serves. Over the years that single hospital has grown into one of South and West India's most respected mid-sized private hospital groups.
Today the Network operates 12 hospitals across Telangana, Karnataka and Maharashtra, supported by more than 40 clinics and diagnostic centres. We are a family of approximately 8,500 staff — doctors, nurses, technicians, billing and administrative colleagues — caring for over two million patient visits every year. Our registered head office remains in Hyderabad, Telangana.

1. About MediAssist Health Network
Welcome
This handbook is your primary onboarding reference. Keep it accessible during your first 90 days. Where this handbook and a specific policy document differ, the specific policy document prevails.

2. Organisational Structure
MediAssist follows a clear governance and reporting hierarchy. Understanding where you sit in this structure helps you know who to approach for approvals, escalations and support.
The reporting hierarchy runs through six levels:
- Board of Directors — governance, strategy and statutory oversight.
- Chief Executive Officer (CEO) — network-wide operations and performance.
- CMO / CTO / CFO — clinical, technology and finance leadership respectively.
- Department Heads (HODs) — management of clinical and non-clinical departments.
- Senior Staff — consultants, senior nurses and unit in-charges.
- Junior Staff — resident doctors, staff nurses, technicians and executives.

2. Organisational Structure
Text-based org chart
Board of Directors
■■ CEO
■■ Chief Medical Officer (CMO)
■ Department Heads ■
Senior Clinical Staff
■
Junior Clinical
- ■■ Chief Technology Officer (CTO) ■ IT & Biomedical Heads ■ Engineers & Technicians
- ■■ Chief Financial Officer (CFO) ■ Billing & Accounts Heads ■ Billing Executives

2. Organisational Structure
Patient First
Every decision is measured against one question: is this good for the patient? Clinical and non-clinical staff alike are expected to place patient safety, dignity and comfort ahead of convenience.

2. Organisational Structure
Integrity
We are honest in our clinical records, our billing, and our dealings with patients, insurers and each other. There is no acceptable shortcut around the truth.

2. Organisational Structure
Clinical Excellence
We practise evidence-based medicine, follow our Standard Treatment Protocols, and continually audit our outcomes against national benchmarks.

2. Organisational Structure
Continuous Learning
Medicine and technology never stand still. We invest in CME, certifications and structured on-the-job training for every grade of staff.
Staff

2. Organisational Structure
Team Respect
A hospital runs on teamwork. We treat colleagues across every department and grade with courtesy, and we have zero tolerance for harassment or abuse.

4. Facilities & Campuses
MediAssist's flagship campuses each carry a clinical focus. New hires are usually assigned to one home campus but may be rotated for training.
- MediAssist Hyderabad Central (Hyderabad, Telangana) — multi-speciality flagship with emergency services.
- MediAssist Secunderabad (Secunderabad, Telangana) — cardiac sciences and critical care.
- MediAssist Bengaluru Onco Centre (Bengaluru, Karnataka) — oncology and radiation therapy.
- MediAssist Pune Speciality (Pune, Maharashtra) — orthopaedics and mother-and-child care.
- MediAssist Mysuru Clinic Hub (Mysuru, Karnataka) — day-care, dialysis and outpatient services.

5. Working Hours & Shifts
Working patterns differ between administrative and clinical staff.
- Standard office hours (administrative, billing, HR, IT): 9:00 am – 6:00 pm, Monday to Saturday, with a one-hour lunch break.
- Clinical staff work three rotating shifts: Morning 7:00 am – 3:00 pm , Afternoon 3:00 pm – 11:00 pm, and Night 11:00 pm – 7:00 am .

5. Working Hours & Shifts
Weekly off policy
- Administrative staff: one fixed weekly off (Sunday) plus alternate Saturdays.
- Clinical staff: one rotational weekly off scheduled by the unit in-charge, ensuring no employee works more than six consecutive days.

metadata.section_title: 5. Working Hours & Shifts > Weekly off policy

text:
6. Staff ID & Access Cards
- Issuance: Your photo ID and RFID access card are issued by the Facilities desk on day one, against a signed acknowledgement.
- Lost card procedure: Report a lost card to the Facilities desk and IT Security immediately. The card is deactivated within 30 minutes; a replacement is issued within two working days against a ₹250 replacement fee.
- Access zones by role: Cards are zoned — clinical areas (ICU, OT, wards) are restricted to clinical staff and authorised personnel; server rooms to IT; pharmacy stores to pharmacy staff. Never lend your card or tailgate.

7. Dress Code
Uniforms identify roles at a glance and must be worn with a visible photo ID:
- Doctors — white coat over blue scrubs.
- Nurses — green scrubs, closed-toe footwear, hair tied back.
- Technicians — grey uniform with safety footwear where required.
- Administrative & Billing staff — formal business attire.

8. Grievance Redressal
MediAssist is committed to resolving staff grievances fairly and promptly.
- Raise a grievance with your immediate supervisor first; if unresolved or inappropriate, approach the HR Business Partner for your campus.
- Every formal grievance is acknowledged within 3 working days .
- Resolution is targeted within 21 days of acknowledgement; complex cases are communicated with an interim status.
- Grievances may be raised confidentially; retaliation is itself a disciplinary offence.

9. IT & Systems Access
- Requesting access: Raise a request on the IT Service Desk portal, approved by your HOD. Access is provisioned on the principle of least privilege.
- Acceptable use: MediAssist systems are for clinical and business use. No unauthorised software, no personal data storage on hospital devices, no circumventing security controls.
- Password policy: Minimum 12 characters with mixed case, a number and a symbol; rotate every 90 days; never share or reuse across systems. Multi-factor authentication is mandatory for clinical and billing systems.

9. IT & Systems Access
Security
Never share your login credentials, even with a colleague or supervisor. Suspected account compromise must be reported to IT Security within 2 hours.

10. Probation & Confirmation
All new permanent employees serve a probation period during which performance, conduct and fit are assessed.
- Probation period: 6 months for clinical staff; 3 months for non-clinical staff.
- Probation may be extended once, by up to 3 months, with documented feedback.
- Confirmation is issued in writing on successful completion; benefits such as study leave accrue from the confirmation date.
- During probation the notice period for either party is 15 days.

11. Training & Professional Development
- Every clinical employee completes mandatory induction training in BLS (Basic Life Support), infection control, fire safety and POSH within 30 days of joining.
- Doctors and nurses must accrue Continuing Medical Education (CME) credits annually; study leave (5 days/yr) supports approved certification courses.
- Technicians undergo equipment-specific certification before independent operation of any biomedical device.
- An annual training calendar is published on the HR Portal; nominations are routed through HODs.

12. Emergency Codes
MediAssist uses a standard colour-code system across all campuses. Every employee must memorise these codes irrespective of role:
- Code Blue — adult cardiac or respiratory arrest. Dial 7777; start BLS if trained.
- Code Pink — infant or child abduction. Secure exits and alert security.
- Code Red — fire. Raise the alarm and evacuate via fire exits; never use lifts.
- Code Orange — hazardous material spill. Cordon the area; call facilities and safety.
- Code Grey — violent or aggressive person. Call security and ensure your personal safety.
- Code Yellow — internal or external disaster (mass casualty). Report to your muster point.

13. Key Contacts
- HR Helpdesk — 1800-180-4500 (toll-free) · hr.help@mediassist.in
- IT Support — ithelp@mediassist.in · ext. 2200
- Facilities Desk — facilities@mediassist.in · ext. 1100
- Medical Director's Office — md.office@mediassist.in · ext. 1000
- POSH Committee — posh@mediassist.in
- Emergency (internal) — dial 7777 from any hospital extension.

ICU Nursing Procedures Manual
Standard Operating Procedures for Critical Care Nursing
MediAssist Health Network
Nursing Education & Critical Care
Document ref: NURS-ICU-008 · Version 5.0 Access: Nurses, Doctors, Admin
This manual sets out standard operating procedures (SOPs) for common ICU nursing tasks at MediAssist hospitals. Each SOP lists pre-requisites, numbered steps, monitoring parameters and documentation requirements. Procedures must be performed only by staff assessed as competent for the task.

Frequency
Dressing change every 72 hours, or sooner if the dressing is soiled, loose or no longer occlusive.

Equipment checklist
- Sterile gloves and sterile dressing pack
- Chlorhexidine 2% in 70% alcohol solution
- Sterile drape
- Transparent semi-permeable dressing
- Date label

Procedure
- 1 Perform hand hygiene and don personal protective equipment (PPE).
- 2 Remove the old dressing and discard in the yellow (biomedical) bin.
- 3 Inspect the insertion site for redness, swelling, or discharge.
- 4 Perform hand hygiene again and apply sterile gloves.
- 5 Clean the site with chlorhexidine using a concentric (inside-out) motion.
- 6 Allow to air-dry for at least 30 seconds — do not fan or blow.
- 7 Apply the new transparent dressing.
- 8 Label the dressing with the date and your initials.
- 9 Document the procedure and site assessment in the care record.

Escalate
Notify the doctor immediately for: purulent discharge, fever > 38.5 °C with no other source, or tracking erythema extending > 2 cm from the insertion site.

Initial settings
Mode, Starting Value = Assist-control / Volume-control (AC/VC). Tidal volume, Starting Value = 6–8 mL/kg ideal body weight. Respiratory rate, Starting Value = 12–16 breaths/min. FiO₂, Starting Value = Start 1.0, then titrate to SpO₂ 94–98%. PEEP, Starting Value = 5 cmH₂O (default)

Hourly monitoring
- SpO₂, end-tidal CO₂ (EtCO₂)
- Peak and plateau airway pressures
- Delivered tidal volume
- Ventilator alarm status

Alarm response
High pressure (> 40 cmH₂O), Immediate Action = Check for secretions / kinking / coughing → suction if indicated → call doctor if it persists. Low SpO₂ (< 90%), Immediate Action = Increase FiO₂ → assess airway and circuit → call doctor immediately

Ventilator bundle (VAP prevention)
- Head of bed (HOB) elevation 30–45°
- Oral care with chlorhexidine every 4 hours
- Subglottic suctioning where available
- Daily sedation vacation and readiness-to-wean assessment

Sizing
Adult: 14–16 Fr. Paediatric: select by age-appropriate formula.

Insertion procedure
- 1 Measure from the tip of the nose to the earlobe to the xiphoid process; mark the tube.
- 2 Lubricate the tip with water-soluble gel.
- 3 Insert through the nostril, advancing gently.
- 4 Ask the patient to swallow (sips of water if permitted) as you advance.
- 5 Advance to the measured length.
- 6 Confirm position by aspirate pH < 5.5 AND chest X-ray before first feed.

Feeding & gastric residual monitoring
Initiation, Action = Start at 20 mL/hr. If GRV < 200 mL, Action = Increase by 20 mL/hr every 4 hours. If GRV 200–400 mL, Action = Hold feed 1 hour and recheck. If GRV > 400 mL, Action = Hold feed; notify dietitian and doctor

Site selection (order of preference)
Forearm → antecubital fossa → dorsum of hand. Avoid the legs except in emergency.

Cannula sizing by indication
Blood transfusion, Recommended Gauge = ≥ 18G. Rapid fluid resuscitation, Recommended Gauge = ≥ 16G. Routine medication, Recommended Gauge = 20–22G. Paediatric < 5 kg, Recommended Gauge = 24G. Paediatric 5–20 kg, Recommended Gauge = 22G. Paediatric > 20 kg, Recommended Gauge = 20G

Failed attempt protocol
- Maximum 2 attempts per nurse .
- After 2 failed attempts, escalate to a senior nurse or doctor.

Cannula change
Replace every 72–96 hours, or immediately if there are signs of phlebitis (Visual Infusion Phlebitis [VIP] score ≥ 2).

Principle
Restraint is a last resort to prevent imminent harm (self-extubation, falls) and must never be used as routine care or for staff convenience.

Authorisation
- Requires a verbal doctor's order, with a written order within 1 hour .
- Re-authorisation is required every 24 hours .

Monitoring while restrained
- Neurovascular check every 2 hours — circulation, sensation and movement in the restrained limb.
- Offer range-of-motion exercises every 2 hours.
- Document every check.

Documentation requirements
- Reason for restraint and alternatives tried
- Time applied and type of restraint
- All monitoring observations
- Communication with the family

SOP 6 — Endotracheal Suctioning
Suctioning clears secretions to maintain airway patency in the intubated patient. It is performed only when clinically indicated, not on a fixed routine.

SOP 6 — Endotracheal Suctioning
Indications
- Visible or audible secretions / coarse breath sounds
- Rising peak airway pressure or falling tidal volume
- Desaturation or a sawtooth pattern on the flow-volume loop

SOP 6 — Endotracheal Suctioning
Procedure
- 1 Hand hygiene; don gloves, apron and eye protection.
- 2 Pre-oxygenate with FiO₂ 1.0 for 30–60 seconds.
- 3 Use a closed in-line suction catheter where available; select catheter ≤ half the ETT internal diameter.
- 4 Insert without suction; apply suction on withdrawal for no more than 10–15 seconds.
- 5 Limit to two passes; allow recovery between passes.
- 6 Return ventilator settings to baseline and reassess.
- 7 Document secretion colour, volume and the patient's tolerance.

SOP 6 — Endotracheal Suctioning
Safety
Stop suctioning immediately for SpO₂ < 90%, bradycardia, or a new arrhythmia, and re-oxygenate the patient.

SOP 6 — Endotracheal Suctioning
Risk assessment
Assess every ICU patient using the Braden Scale on admission and at least every 24 hours. A score ≤ 18 indicates increased risk.

SOP 6 — Endotracheal Suctioning
Preventive bundle
- Repositioning — turn the patient every 2 hours and document the position.
- Support surface — use a pressure-redistributing mattress for high-risk patients.
- Skin inspection — inspect bony prominences each shift.
- Moisture management — keep the skin clean and dry; apply barrier cream as needed.
- Nutrition — refer at-risk patients to the dietitian.

SOP 6 — Endotracheal Suctioning
Staging quick reference
- Stage 1 — non-blanching erythema with intact skin.
- Stage 2 — partial-thickness skin loss; a shallow open ulcer.
- Stage 3 — full-thickness skin loss with fat visible.
- Stage 4 — full-thickness tissue loss with muscle or bone visible.

Infection Control Guidelines
Standard Precautions and Outbreak Management
MediAssist Health Network
Hospital Infection Control Committee
Document ref: NURS-IC-009 · Version 4.2 Access: Nurses, Doctors, Admin
These guidelines define the standard precautions and outbreak-management procedures that apply across all MediAssist facilities. Adherence is mandatory for all clinical staff and is monitored by the Infection Control team.

1. Five Moments of Hand Hygiene
The WHO 'Five Moments' framework is the foundation of infection prevention. Perform hand hygiene at each of these moments:
- 1 Before patient contact — to protect the patient from your hands.
- 2 Before an aseptic procedure — to protect the patient from infection.
- 3 After body-fluid exposure risk — to protect yourself and the environment.
- 4 After patient contact — to protect yourself and the environment.
- 5 After contact with patient surroundings — to protect yourself and the environment.

1. Five Moments of Hand Hygiene
Technique
Duration: alcohol-based hand rub 20–30 seconds; soap-and-water handwash 40–60 seconds. Wash with soap and water when hands are visibly soiled or after caring for a patient with C. difficile.

metadata.section_title: 1. Five Moments of Hand Hygiene > Technique

text:
2. PPE Selection Guide
Routine patient contact, Gloves = Yes. Routine patient contact, Apron/Gown = No. Routine patient contact, Mask = No. Routine patient contact, Eye Protection = No. Contact with blood/body fluids, Gloves = Yes. Contact with blood/body fluids, Apron/Gown = Yes. Contact with blood/body fluids, Mask = Surgical. Contact with blood/body fluids, Eye Protection = If splash risk. Suspected respiratory infection, Gloves = Yes. Suspected respiratory infection, Apron/Gown = Yes. Suspected respiratory infection, Mask = N95. Suspected respiratory infection, Eye Protection = Goggles/shield. Aerosol-generating procedure, Gloves = Yes. Aerosol-generating procedure, Apron/Gown = Full gown. Aerosol-generating procedure, Mask = N95/PAPR. Aerosol-generating procedure, Eye Protection = Full face shield. Isolation room (contact), Gloves = Yes. Isolation room (contact), Apron/Gown = Yes. Isolation room (contact), Mask = Surgical. Isolation room (contact), Eye Protection = As indicated
Aerosol-generating procedures include intubation, extubation, bronchoscopy, open suctioning and non-invasive ventilation.

3. Standard Precautions
Standard precautions apply to all patients regardless of diagnosis. They treat blood and all body fluids, secretions and excretions (except sweat), non-intact skin, and mucous membranes as potentially infectious. They include hand hygiene, appropriate PPE, safe injection practice, and safe handling of sharps and linen.

4. Transmission-Based Precautions
Contact, Example Organisms = MRSA, VRE, C. difficile, norovirus. Contact, Key Measures = Single room preferred; dedicated equipment; visitors gown + gloves. Droplet, Example Organisms = Influenza, pertussis, meningococcus. Droplet, Key Measures = Surgical mask within 1 metre; single room preferred. Airborne, Example Organisms = TB, measles, chickenpox. Airborne, Key Measures = Negative-pressure room; N95; limit patient transport

5. Healthcare-Associated Infection (HAI) Surveillance
The four HAIs under active surveillance are:
- CAUTI — catheter-associated urinary tract infection.
- CLABSI — central line-associated bloodstream infection.
- VAP — ventilator-associated pneumonia.
- SSI — surgical site infection.
- Any suspected HAI must be reported to the infection control nurse within 24 hours .
- A root-cause analysis (RCA) is conducted for every confirmed HAI, with learning shared at the monthly infection control committee.

6. Outbreak Management Protocol
An outbreak is defined as two or more linked cases within 72 hours .

6. Outbreak Management Protocol
Notification chain
Ward in-charge → Infection Control team → Chief Medical Officer (CMO).

6. Outbreak Management Protocol
Control measures
- Cohort affected patients; dedicate staff and equipment where possible.
- Implement visitor restrictions.
- Enhance environmental cleaning frequency and audit.
- Issue a staff-briefing communication using the standard template.

7. Waste Segregation
Yellow, Waste Type = Biomedical / infectious. Yellow, Examples = Dressings, blood-soaked items, body fluids. Red, Waste Type = Anatomical / contaminated plastics. Red, Examples = Tubing, catheters, IV sets. Blue / White (translucent), Waste Type = Glassware & sharps. Blue / White (translucent), Examples = Vials, ampoules, needles (puncture-proof). Black, Waste Type = General (non-hazardous). Black, Examples = Office and domestic waste. White (cytotoxic), Waste Type = Cytotoxic / pharmaceutical. White (cytotoxic), Examples = Chemotherapy waste

7. Waste Segregation
Practice
Segregate at the point of generation. Biomedical waste bins are removed at least once per shift, and more frequently when ¾ full. Document collection in the waste register.

8. Needlestick & Sharps Injury Management
A sharps injury is a medical emergency for the exposed worker and must be acted on immediately.
- 1 Encourage the wound to bleed gently; wash with soap and running water (do not scrub).
- 2 Do not suck the wound; for splashes to eyes/mouth, irrigate with water or saline.
- 3 Report to the shift in-charge and attend the Emergency department / occupational health without delay .
- 4 Identify the source patient; risk-assess for HIV, Hepatitis B and Hepatitis C.
- 5 Commence post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) if indicated — ideally within 2 hours, and no later than 72 hours for HIV exposure.
- 6 Complete the incident report and arrange baseline and follow-up serology.

8. Needlestick & Sharps Injury Management
Prevention
Never recap needles. Dispose of sharps at the point of use in the puncture-proof sharps container, filled no more than three-quarters.

9. Antimicrobial Stewardship (nursing role)
- Administer antimicrobials on time; delays compromise efficacy and resistance control.
- Prompt review of IV-to-oral switch when the patient is improving and afebrile.
- Ensure cultures are sent before the first antibiotic dose where feasible.
- Flag to the medical team any antibiotic continued beyond review date or without a documented indication.

10. Isolation Signage
Colour-coded door signs tell staff and visitors which precautions are in force before they enter:
- Contact (yellow sign) — contact precautions in force; gown and gloves before entry.
- Droplet (blue sign) — droplet precautions in force; surgical mask within 1 metre.
- Airborne (red sign) — airborne precautions in force; N95 plus a negative-pressure room.
